topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the mail slot clattering was the first thing that snagged my attention. Another junk flyer, another bill, another piece of unsolicited paper cluttering the entryway. I dragged myself to the door, bracing for the usual barrage of unwanted correspondence. Then, tucked amongst the glossy advertisements, something caught my eye: a thick, cream-colored card, completely blank on the front. My brow furrowed immediately. Who sends an empty card? I picked it up, the paper feeling strangely soft beneath my fingertips. A sharp sigh escaped my lips as I scanned the address, my jaw tightening. It was unsigned. I crumpled it instinctively, the crisp edges protesting under my grip. "What in the world?" I muttered to the empty hallway, shaking my head.

[story 2]

I was already on edge. The persistent buzzing of the phone, the stack of unopened envelopes threatening to topple over, the general sense of domestic overload—it all contributed to a low, simmering tension in my chest. When I finally reached for the mail, it felt like an imposition. There, lying awkwardly beside a utility statement, was a card. It was pristine, folded neatly, bearing no name or message. My eyes narrowed as I picked it up, my knuckles whitening around the cardstock. A strange prickle of annoyance ran down my spine. Why would someone leave something so deliberately ambiguous? I flipped it over, my gaze sweeping across the bare surface. I let out a short, sharp exhale, a sound of pure exasperation, and tossed it onto the nearest desk, hoping the oddity would vanish.

[story 3]

The afternoon light streaming through the window did nothing to alleviate the tightness in my shoulders. Everything felt too loud, too demanding today. I was trying to focus on sorting through the day's paperwork, but my concentration kept fracturing. Then, I noticed it—a card tucked into the slot. It was a birthday card, clearly, but utterly devoid of any inscription or sender identification. My movements became jerky as I reached for it. A deep frown settled onto my face, pulling the corners of my mouth down. I held the card up to the light, scrutinizing the plain white expanse. It felt like a deliberate snub. "Seriously?" I whispered, the disbelief laced with a distinct weariness. I dropped it back into the box with a frustrated shove, wishing the whole situation would just dissolve.
